attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for follow-up of worsening anxiety, irritability, and insomnia occurring in the week before and during her menses. She also experiences bloating and increased appetite during this time. These symptoms significantly impact her work and family life. She describes feeling worthless and hopeless during her last period, and that these feelings really scared her, as she generally enjoys life and loves her roles as a mother, wife and her professional role as a paramedic. She has a two-year-old child and she and her husband have plans to try to conceive again in about six months time.

At her previous appointment six weeks ago, she was advised on lifestyle modifications. She has reduced caffeine and alcohol intake, increased physical activity through daily walks, and improved her diet with guidance from a dietician. Despite these changes, her symptoms persist. She has attempted meditation but found it difficult to maintain.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her mental state examination is unremarkable today, though she describes significant distress during the premenstrual period.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Luteal phase selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms consistent with premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD). PMDD is diagnosed when at least five symptoms occur for two or more menstrual cycles, with at least one being mood-related (*). Symptoms typically occur during the luteal phase and resolve with menses. Symptoms can include:

  • *Intense or sudden shifts in mood

  • *Increased irritability or frequent anger

  • *Persistent low mood, including feelings of hopelessness, guilt, or worthlessness

  • *Heightened anxiety

  • Anhedonia

  • Trouble concentrating

  • Fatigue, low energy levels

  • Changes in eating patterns

  • Insomnia

  • Physical complaints such as breast pain or swelling, bloating, weight gain, muscle or joint aches, acne, and headaches

First line treatment for PMDD involves non-pharmacological management including increasing exercise (especially aerobic exercise such as walking, running or swimming), reducing caffeine and alcohol intake, dietary modification (including increasing fruit and vegetables, healthy fats, good quality protein and complex carbohydrates such as whole grains and quinoa - input of a dietician can be useful), reducing stress levels and psychological support. In this case the patient has already attempted lifestyle modification measures and has severe symptoms that warrants consideration of pharmacological support.

Luteal phase selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) therapy is the most appropriate next step, given the severity of symptoms and failure of lifestyle modifications. This involves taking the medication only during the luteal phase (typically starting 14 days before menses and continuing until menstruation begins). Interestingly, patients with PMDD often respond to much lower doses of SSRIs than that used for major depressive disorder and respond rapidly to treatment with intermittent dosing. This approach minimises medication exposure while providing effective symptom control.

Although daily SSRI therapy can be effective for patients with PMDD using these medications on a continuous basis exposes the patient to unnecessary medication during the follicular phase when symptoms are typically absent. The luteal phase dosing strategy is preferred for most patients, particularly given her plans for future pregnancy. The use of other classes of antidepressants including serotonin-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs) are typically not recommended as first line therapy due to reduced evidence base compared to SSRIs, however venlafaxine can be considered as a second line option for non-responders to luteal phase SSRI therapy.

Combined oral contraceptive pill can be effective for PMDD but would not be appropriate given her desire to conceive in the near future. Additionally, some women experience mood-related side effects from hormonal contraception.

Some people with PMDD also experience benefit from vitamin and herbal supplements including calcium supplementation, vitamin B6 supplementation and evening primrose oil. However, given the severity of this patient's symptoms, more definitive treatment is warranted in this case.
learningPoints: A diagnosis of premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) requires at least five symptoms including mood-related symptoms for two or more menstrual cycles.
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) taken during the luteal phase are first-line pharmacological treatment for premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) after lifestyle measures.
Treatment choices for premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) should consider future pregnancy planning.
references: Steiner M, Pearlstein T, Cohen LS, Endicott J, Kornstein SG, Roberts C, et al. Expert guidelines for the treatment of severe PMS, PMDD, and comorbidities: the role of SSRIs. J Womens Health (Larchmt). 2006;15(1):57–69. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1089/jwh.2006.15.57

Freeman EW, Rickels K, Sondheimer SJ, Polansky M, Xiao S. Continuous or intermittent dosing with sertraline for patients with severe premenstrual syndrome or premenstrual dysphoric disorder. Am J Psychiatry. 2004;161(2):343–51. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1176/appi.ajp.161.2.343

Landén M, Nissbrandt H, Allgulander C, Sörvik K, Ysander C, Eriksson E. Placebo-controlled trial comparing intermittent and continuous paroxetine in premenstrual dysphoric disorder. Neuropsychopharmacology. 2007 Jan;32(1):153-61. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17035933/

Carlini SV, Lanza di Scalea T, McNally ST, Lester J, Deligiannidis KM. Management of Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder: A Scoping Review. Int J Womens Health. 2022 Dec 21;14:1783-1801. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9790166/
